region_of_origin: Northwest China (Xinjiang / Gansu influence); served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for lamb tossed noodles; FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FHR covers cooked pasta and noodles as a category; no specific lamb noodle composite code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229953004 represents noodles as a food concept; no specific lamb noodle dish term exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of lamb noodles is approximately 380 g, comprising a generous portion of wheat noodles topped with braised or stir-fried lamb slices and sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb and noodle composite estimated from USDA FoodData Central: Lamb, domestic, composite of trimmed retail cuts, cooked (FDC ID 174382) and Noodles, Chinese, chow mein, dry (FDC ID 169737), combined in approximate 35:65 lamb-to-noodle ratio with sauce allowance
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for lamb tossed noodles exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA Foundation Foods, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values are estimated by combining USDA FDC data for cooked lamb (FDC ID 174382) and cooked wheat noodles (FDC ID 169737) in a 35:65 ratio by cooked weight, with an additional allowance for oil and soy sauce seasoning. Energy and macros are cross-checked against published Chinese restaurant noodle dish ranges in peer-reviewed dietary survey literature.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — wheat noodles, boiled (GI ~47, analogous estimate for mixed lamb noodle dish)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lamb tossed noodles. GI of 47 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled wheat noodles (GI ~47, low category), which represents the dominant glycemic component of this dish. The lamb and oil components do not contribute meaningfully to glycemic response. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (47 × 81.7 g carbohydrate in 380 g serving) / 100 ≈ 38.3. This is a high GL per serving due to large noodle portion size, consistent with a restaurant-sized plate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1596 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced soy sauce, as a single restaurant serving may contribute approximately 70–80% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (GL ~38) despite low GI of the noodle base (GI 47)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that the large noodle portion produces a high glycemic load per serving; reducing portion size to approximately half a plate and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from lamb (~1.8 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the combination of saturated fat from lamb and high sodium from soy-based seasoning warrants portion control; choosing leaner lamb cuts and requesting lighter sauce can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Noodle with Lamb (羊肉拌面, Yángròu bàn miàn) is a Chinese noodle dish featuring wheat noodles tossed with cooked lamb slices and a savoury sauce typically made from soy sauce, garlic, chilli, sesame oil, and spring onion. The dish has strong culinary roots in Northwest China, particularly the Xinjiang and Gansu regions, where lamb is a dietary staple among Muslim communities, and it is served in Singapore at specialist Chinese Muslim and Northwest Chinese restaurants such as Bei Jiang Restaurant. The noodles are boiled until tender and then tossed — rather than served in broth — giving the dish a dry, well-coated texture with a bold umami flavour, mild heat, and a fragrant sesame aroma. The lamb contributes a distinctive gamey richness that is balanced by the aromatic sauce. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein (~9.8 g per 100 g) from lamb, a significant carbohydrate load from wheat noodles (~21.5 g per 100 g), and moderate fat (~5.2 g per 100 g). A full restaurant serving of approximately 380 g delivers around 638 kcal. The dish is naturally halal and dairy-free but contains gluten and soy allergens. Clinically, the high sodium content (~1596 mg per serving) is a concern for hypertension management, and the high glycemic load per serving (GL ~38) requires portion awareness for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, despite the noodle base having a low GI of approximately 47.
common_user_queries: Is lamb noodle good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for lamb tossed noodles was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for cooked lamb and cooked wheat noodles combined in a representative ratio, with sauce allowance. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled wheat noodles. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated composites; no exact approved-source entry for this dish exists. Values should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis or a future HPB Singapore Foodo entry if one becomes available. 2. Vitamin D is set to -1 as it is not meaningfully reported for this dish type in the USDA FoodData Central source data and is expected to be negligible. 3. Glycemic index of 47 is an analogous estimate based on boiled wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for lamb tossed noodles was located in the published literature. 4. Sodium estimate (~420 mg per 100 g) reflects typical soy-sauce-seasoned Chinese noodle dishes and may vary substantially by restaurant preparation. 5. The dish is served at Bei Jiang Restaurant (北疆饭店), a Northwest Chinese restaurant in Singapore, and is priced at SGD 18.75 per the field survey source record. 6. The dish is halal-suitable given lamb is used and no pork or alcohol ingredients are typical in this preparation style, though individual restaurant certification should be verified. 7. All -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg) indicate the nutrient is not reported in the source database for this food type.
